docs.title: The Right Way (Container Orchestration)
docs.text:
Instead of rebuilding everything, we orchestrate existing working containers:

Your Working Medusa (Tor)
    +
Official I2P Container
    +
Official Yggdrasil Container
    +
HAProxy Smart Router
    =
NeXuS Multi-Network Stack

docs.title: 1. nexus-compose.yaml (Container Orchestration)
docs.text:
• Uses your existing working medusa-proxy
  • Adds official I2P container (purplei2p/i2pd)
  • Adds official Yggdrasil container
  • HAProxy routes between them
  • Everything isolated, nothing breaks

docs.title: 2. haproxy-nexus.cfg (Smart Routing)
docs.text:
• Routes *.i2p domains → I2P network
  • Routes everything else → Tor (your medusa)
  • Stats page on port 9090
  • Simple, understandable config

docs.title: 3. nexus-start.sh (One Command Startup)
docs.text:
• Starts all containers with one command
  • Shows all proxy endpoints
  • Gives test commands
  • Shows how to stop

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
cd /home/user/git/medusa-proxy

# Start the NeXuS stack
./nexus-start.sh

# Proxy endpoints:
# - Main Router: localhost:9080 (SOCKS) - smart routing
# - HTTP Router: localhost:9443 (HTTP) - domain-based routing
# - Tor Direct:  localhost:1080 (your existing medusa)
# - I2P Direct:  localhost:4447 (I2P SOCKS)
# - Stats:       http://localhost:9090 (admin/nexus)

# Test Tor routing
curl --socks5 localhost:9080 http://check.torproject.org/api/ip

# Test I2P access
curl --socks5 localhost:4447 http://example.i2p

# Stop everything
podman-compose -f nexus-compose.yaml down

docs.text:

docs.title: Production Ready

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Client Application
       ↓
HAProxy Router (localhost:9080)
       ↓
    ┌──┴──┐
    ↓     ↓
  Tor    I2P    Yggdrasil
(medusa) ↓      ↓
 1080   4447   9001

docs.title: Adding More Networks
docs.text:
Want to add more? Just edit nexus-compose.yaml:

  reticulum:
    image: reticulum/rnsd:latest
    container_name: nexus-reticulum
    ports:
      - "4965:4965"
    networks:
      - nexus-net

Then update haproxy-nexus.cfg to route to it.

docs.title: Containers won't start
docs.text:

podman ps
netstat -tlnp | grep -E "(9080|1080|4447)"

# View logs
podman-compose -f nexus-compose.yaml logs

curl --socks5 localhost:4447 http://example.i2p  # I2P

To join NeXuS network, you MUST contribute % of your system: 1. Storage - % of disk space (default 10%) 2. Bandwidth - % of network capacity (Tor relay, I2P router) 3. CPU Cycles - % of processing power (distributed jobs) 4. Routing - Packet forwarding (mesh networking)

Example: 

You have: 500GB disk, 100 Mbps internet, 4-core CPU
You contribute: 10% = 50GB storage, 10 Mbps bandwidth, 0.4 CPU cores
You earn: NeXuS tokens (based on contribution % + network state)

Traditional Cloud: Pay $10/month forever (money) NeXuS: Pay 10% of your system resources (entry price)

docs.title: Self-Regulating Algorithm
docs.text:
def calculate_daily_token_release(network_state):
    total_nodes = network_state.node_count
    total_resources = network_state.storage_gb + network_state.bandwidth_mbps
    network_usage = network_state.transactions_per_day

    base_release = sqrt(total_nodes * total_resources)
    usage_multiplier = log(network_usage + 1)

    return base_release * usage_multiplier

def calculate_token_value(network_state, circulating_supply):
    network_asset_value = (
        network_state.storage_gb * storage_market_rate +
        network_state.bandwidth_tb * bandwidth_market_rate +
        network_state.cpu_hours * cpu_market_rate
    )
    return network_asset_value / circulating_supply

Why "Bass Akwards" Works: - Early adopters: Scarce tokens, high value - Later adopters: More tokens, but still valuable - ALWAYS supports adoption (never "too late to join") - Self-regulating (no central bank)

NeXuS Network Fund: - Small % from transactions (2-5%) - Funds partnerships, development, operations - Governed by Town Hall (DAO voting) - Crypto-managed, transparent

This Creates SURGE in Adoption: - Router works EVERYWHERE (any device, any OS) - Low barrier = mass adoption - Network effects (more routers = better routing) - Proven value → Natural upgrade to full OS

docs.title: 3. Stress Testing (CRITICAL - Lives Depend On It)
docs.text:
Goal: Break it BEFORE users do

Test Scenarios:

a) High Load - 1,000+ concurrent connections - Saturate bandwidth - Max out CPU routing decisions - Find breaking point

b) Network Chaos - Kill Tor randomly (does failover work?) - Kill I2P mid-transfer (does it recover?) - Kill ALL networks then bring back (does it heal?) - Rapid cycling (stress failover logic)

c) Peer Churn - Nodes joining/leaving constantly - Mesh network instability - B.A.T.M.A.N./Yggdrasil peer discovery under chaos

d) Latency Hell - Simulate 5000ms latency (Tor over satellite) - Network congestion, packet loss - Does health monitoring handle slow networks?

e) DDoS Simulation - Flood with requests - Resource exhaustion - Memory leaks (run 24 hours)

f) Edge Cases - Malformed requests - Unknown protocols - Circular routing (A→B→A loops) - DNS poisoning attempts

g) AI Decision Testing - Test pattern recognition accuracy - Emergency fallback (kill internet → Meshtastic?) - AI routing under load - Verify no AI decision causes failure

Tools: - ab (Apache Bench) - HTTP load testing - iperf3 - Bandwidth saturation - tc (traffic control) - Simulate latency/packet loss - Custom chaos scripts - Kill networks randomly - stress-ng - CPU/memory exhaustion

Success Criteria: - Handles 1,000+ connections without crash - Failover works under load - Recovers from all network failures - No memory leaks (24-hour run) - Graceful degradation (slow, not broken) - AI decisions don't cause failures

docs.title: The True Vision: Bitcoin + Plan 9 + Napster
NeXuS Completes What They Started
docs.text:
Three revolutionary technologies. Three visions of freedom. All corrupted or killed.

NeXuS brings them back - and finishes the job.

docs.title: Bitcoin (2009): Decentralized Money
docs.text:
Satoshi's Vision:

"A purely peer-to-peer version of electronic cash would allow online payments to be sent directly from one party to another without going through a financial institution."

Key Principles: - No central authority - Cryptographic proof, not trust - Peer-to-peer transactions - Pseudonymous (not tied to real identity) - Be your own bank

What Happened: - ✅ Technology succeeded (blockchain works!) - ❌ Vision failed (centralized exchanges, KYC, speculation) - ❌ Banks won (Coinbase = bank with extra steps) - ❌ Governments won (KYC/AML on every exchange) - ❌ Users lost (fees, surveillance, no privacy)

Why It Failed: - Fiat on-ramps require KYC (government pressure) - Exchanges became banks (centralization creeps back) - Scalability issues (Lightning = trusted hubs) - Became investment, not currency (HODLing culture)

docs.title: Plan 9 (1992): Distributed Computing
docs.text:
Bell Labs Vision:

"Your CPU can be in California, your storage in New York, your display in Tokyo. The network makes it all one system."

Key Principles: - Everything is a file - Network transparency - Distributed by design - Simple, elegant, composable - No central servers

What Happened: - ✅ Technology succeeded (9P protocol, ideas spread) - ❌ Adoption failed (too radical for corporate IT) - ❌ Ideas stolen (cloud companies = Plan 9 + surveillance) - ❌ Users lost (AWS/Google = Plan 9 philosophy, centralized control)

Why It Failed: - Too different from Unix (scared users away) - No killer app (technically superior ≠ market success) - Poor timing (1992 networks too slow) - No commercial backing (AT&T breakup)

docs.title: Napster (1999): Peer-to-Peer Sharing
docs.text:
Shawn Fanning's Vision:

"Your music collection is my music collection. Share everything with everyone. No central storage, just a community of peers."

Key Principles: - Peer-to-peer file sharing - No central storage (just index) - Everyone contributes - Community collaboration - Information wants to be free

What Happened: - ✅ Technology succeeded (P2P works!) - ❌ Legally destroyed (RIAA lawsuit, 2001) - ❌ Replaced by centralization (iTunes, Spotify) - ❌ Users lost (subscriptions instead of ownership) - ❌ Sharing became "piracy" (criminalized)

Why It Failed: - Central index = single point of failure (legal target) - Record labels sued it to death - Replaced with "legal" alternatives (DRM, streaming) - Users traded freedom for convenience

docs.title: How Centralized Powers Kill Decentralization
docs.text:
Stage 1: Innovation Emerges - Bitcoin: Decentralized money invented - Plan 9: Distributed computing demonstrated - Napster: Peer-to-peer sharing works

Stage 2: Threat Recognized - Bitcoin: Banks see threat to their monopoly - Plan 9: Corporate IT sees threat to control - Napster: Record labels see threat to revenue

Stage 3: Attack or Ignore - Bitcoin: Can't shut down (no central server) → capture it instead - Plan 9: Too radical → ignore it, steal ideas later - Napster: Central index → easy legal target → shut down

Stage 4: Centralization Restored - Bitcoin → Centralized exchanges (Coinbase, Binance) + KYC - Plan 9 → Cloud computing (AWS, Google) + surveillance - Napster → Streaming services (Spotify, Apple) + subscriptions

Stage 5: Users Lose - Bitcoin users: Surveillance, fees, no privacy - Plan 9 users: Vendor lock-in, no freedom - Napster users: No ownership, monthly fees

docs.title: Why They All Failed (Partially or Completely)
Common Weaknesses
docs.text: Technology Single Point of Failure Captured By Result Bitcoin Fiat on-ramps (exchanges) Banks/Government KYC surveillance Plan 9 Commercial adoption Corporate IT Ideas stolen, freedom lost Napster Central index server Record labels Shut down, replaced

docs.title: The Lesson
docs.text:
You can't have PARTIAL decentralization.

  • Bitcoin: Decentralized ledger, centralized on-ramps = FAILED
  • Plan 9: Distributed computing, centralized adoption = FAILED
  • Napster: Peer-to-peer files, central index = FAILED

ONE central point = single point of failure = single point of attack.

docs.title: What NeXuS Takes from Each
docs.text:
From Bitcoin: - ✅ Cryptographic identity (no trust needed) - ✅ Decentralized by design (no central authority) - ✅ Peer-to-peer architecture - ❌ AVOID: Centralized on-ramps, KYC chokepoints

From Plan 9: - ✅ Distributed computing philosophy - ✅ Network transparency (mesh is seamless) - ✅ Everything through one interface - ❌ AVOID: Requiring total replacement, no migration path

From Napster: - ✅ Peer-to-peer sharing (everyone contributes) - ✅ Community collaboration - ✅ "Together Everyone Achieves More" - ❌ AVOID: Central index (single point of failure)

docs.title: Bitcoin's Decentralization
docs.text:

- I2P addresses = cryptographic addresses

- No central authority, no KYC

docs.title: How NeXuS Succeeds Where They Failed
Bitcoin's Failure: Centralized On-Ramps
docs.text:
Problem: Need exchanges to buy Bitcoin → KYC → surveillance

NeXuS Solution: - No fiat on-ramps needed (you already have internet) - Start with mesh networks (free, no KYC) - Optional: Buy VPS with Bitcoin (no KYC VPS providers exist) - Optional: Run at home (no external dependency)

Result: No chokepoint to capture.

docs.title: Plan 9's Failure: Too Radical
docs.text:
Problem: Required abandoning Unix, scared users away

NeXuS Solution: - Use existing tools (browser, apps) - Proxy-based (transparent to applications) - Incremental adoption (start simple, grow) - Familiar interface (CLI-first, then lean Wayland)

Result: Migration path, not revolution.

docs.title: Napster's Failure: Central Index
docs.text:
Problem: Central server = single point of attack → shut down

NeXuS Solution: - No central index (I2P DHT, Tor hidden services) - No central servers (fully distributed) - No single point of failure (multi-network routing) - No legal target (no central organization to sue)

Result: Cannot be shut down.

docs.title: What Bitcoin Promised: Financial Sovereignty
docs.text:
"Be your own bank"

NeXuS delivers: - ✅ Be your own ISP (run your own mesh node) - ✅ Be your own cloud (self-hosted services) - ✅ Be your own network (Yggdrasil mesh)

docs.title: What Plan 9 Promised: Distributed Computing
docs.text:
"Your resources can be anywhere"

NeXuS delivers: - ✅ Browser on VPS (CPU anywhere) - ✅ Files in I2P DHT (storage anywhere) - ✅ Phone/laptop (display anywhere) - ✅ All via mesh (network transparent)

docs.title: What Napster Promised: Peer-to-Peer Sharing
docs.text:
"Everyone shares with everyone"

NeXuS delivers: - ✅ Tor relay (share bandwidth) - ✅ I2P router (share storage) - ✅ Snowflake proxy (help censored users) - ✅ "Together Everyone Achieves More"

docs.title: 1. No Single Point of Failure
docs.text:
Bitcoin: Exchanges = single point → captured
Plan 9: Commercial adoption = single point → ignored
Napster: Central index = single point → shut down

NeXuS: FULLY DISTRIBUTED
- No exchanges (mesh is the network)
- No commercial dependency (community driven)
- No central index (I2P DHT, Tor hidden services)

docs.title: 2. Incremental Adoption
docs.text:
Plan 9: Required total replacement → failed
Bitcoin: Works today, but KYC hurts adoption

NeXuS: Start anywhere
- Step 1: Use as proxy (works with everything)
- Step 2: Add remote browser (familiar)
- Step 3: Add services (grow over time)

docs.title: 3. Solves Real Problems NOW
docs.text:
Bitcoin: "Banks are bad" (true, but abstract)
Plan 9: "Distributed is elegant" (true, but academic)
Napster: "Music should be free" (true, but entitled)

NeXuS: "Censorship is here, surveillance is now"
- Governments blocking Tor (China, Iran, Russia)
- ISPs monitoring everything (USA, UK, Australia)
- Privacy dying (real, immediate threat)

docs.title: 4. Perfect Timing
docs.text:
Bitcoin (2009): Financial crisis, distrust of banks → RIGHT TIME
Plan 9 (1992): No fast networks, no need yet → WRONG TIME
Napster (1999): Broadband spreading, but music cheap → NEUTRAL

NeXuS (2026): PERFECT TIME
- Privacy crisis (surveillance everywhere)
- Censorship increasing (Tor blocked, VPNs banned)
- Fast networks (gigabit common, mesh viable)
- Awareness high (people understand privacy now)

docs.title: 5. Community Model
docs.text:
Bitcoin: No leader (Satoshi disappeared) → good!
Plan 9: Bell Labs (corporate) → bad
Napster: Shawn Fanning (one person) → sued to death

NeXuS: "Together Everyone Achieves More"
- Open source (GPL)
- Community driven (no single leader)
- Cannot be sued (no corporation)
- Cannot be shut down (no servers to seize)

docs.title: Bitcoin's Promise
docs.text:
"A purely peer-to-peer electronic cash system"

Bitcoin gave us decentralized money. But they captured the on-ramps.

NeXuS gives us decentralized EVERYTHING.

docs.title: Plan 9's Promise
docs.text:
"Distributed computing for everyone"

Plan 9 gave us the architecture. But corporations stole it and added surveillance.

NeXuS gives us distributed computing with PRIVACY.

docs.title: Napster's Promise
docs.text:
"Everyone shares with everyone"

Napster gave us peer-to-peer sharing. But they killed it and sold us subscriptions.

NeXuS gives us peer-to-peer infrastructure.

docs.title: Pillar 1: Bitcoin's Decentralization
docs.text:
No central authority
Cryptographic proof
Peer-to-peer architecture
Trustless by design

Applied to:
- Tor routing (no central servers)
- I2P storage (distributed hash table)
- Yggdrasil mesh (self-organizing)

docs.title: Pillar 2: Plan 9's Distribution
docs.text:
Everything is a file
Network transparency
Resources anywhere
Simple, elegant design

Applied to:
- Browser on VPS (CPU server)
- Files in DHT (file server)
- Phone/laptop (terminal)
- Mesh connects all (9P → Tor/I2P/Ygg)

docs.title: Pillar 3: Napster's Peer-to-Peer
docs.text:
Everyone shares with everyone
Community collaboration
No central storage
Together we're stronger

Applied to:
- Tor relays (everyone routes)
- I2P routers (everyone stores)
- Snowflake (everyone helps)
- "Together Everyone Achieves More"

docs.title: Phase 1: Foundation ✅ (Bitcoin-style decentralization)
docs.text:
COMPLETED:
- Tor routing (3 instances, load balanced)
- I2P network (distributed storage)
- Yggdrasil mesh (encrypted IPv6)
- B.A.T.M.A.N. Advanced (ad-hoc mesh)
- Smart routing (domain/protocol aware)
- No central servers, fully distributed

docs.title: Phase 2: Distribution (Plan 9-style computing)
docs.text:
IN PROGRESS:
- Remote browser (VPS = CPU server)
- I2P DHT storage (distributed file server)
- Phone/laptop (terminal/display)
- Network transparency (mesh routing)

docs.title: Phase 3: Sharing (Napster-style P2P)
docs.text:
PLANNED:
- File sharing (I2P DHT)
- Service sharing (Tor hidden services)
- Bandwidth sharing (relay/router)
- Compute sharing (distributed jobs)

docs.title: Success Metrics: Have We Achieved the Vision?
Bitcoin's Vision: "Be Your Own Bank"
docs.text:
✅ NeXuS: Be your own ISP
   - Run mesh node, route your own traffic
   - No dependency on ISP routing policies

✅ NeXuS: Be your own cloud
   - Self-hosted services (Nextcloud, Jitsi, Matrix)
   - Your data, your control

✅ NeXuS: Be your own network
   - Yggdrasil mesh, B.A.T.M.A.N. ad-hoc
   - No central infrastructure

docs.title: Plan 9's Vision: "Distributed Computing"
docs.text:
✅ NeXuS: CPU anywhere
   - Browser on VPS, desktop on server
   - Compute where it makes sense

✅ NeXuS: Storage anywhere
   - Files in I2P DHT, distributed
   - Accessible from mesh

✅ NeXuS: Display anywhere
   - Phone, laptop, tablet
   - Same session, any device

docs.title: Napster's Vision: "Share Everything"
docs.text:
✅ NeXuS: Share bandwidth
   - Tor relays, I2P routers
   - Everyone contributes

✅ NeXuS: Share storage
   - I2P DHT, IPFS over mesh
   - Distributed file storage

✅ NeXuS: Share compute
   - Distributed jobs (future)
   - Mesh CDN (future)

docs.title: The Future: What Satoshi, Bell Labs, and Napster Dreamed Of
Satoshi's Dream (2009)
docs.text:
"A purely peer-to-peer electronic cash"

→ NeXuS reality: Purely peer-to-peer INFRASTRUCTURE

docs.title: Bell Labs' Dream (1992)
docs.text:
"Distributed computing for everyone"

→ NeXuS reality: Distributed computing + privacy for everyone

docs.title: Napster's Dream (1999)
docs.text:
"Your collection is my collection"

→ NeXuS reality: Your node helps my node, my node helps yours

docs.title: The Synthesis
docs.text:
Bitcoin taught us: Decentralization is possible Plan 9 taught us: Distribution is elegant Napster taught us: Sharing is powerful

NeXuS combines all three:

Decentralized architecture (Bitcoin)
    +
Distributed computing (Plan 9)
    +
Peer-to-peer sharing (Napster)
    +
Privacy by default (NeXuS innovation)
    =
The cloud we should have had

docs.title: Bitcoin: Partially Successful
docs.text:
• ✅ Technology works (blockchain)
  • ❌ Vision corrupted (centralized exchanges)
  • 📊 Score: 50% success

docs.title: Plan 9: Failed Commercially, Succeeded Technically
docs.text:
• ✅ Technology brilliant (9P, UTF-8, etc.)
  • ❌ Adoption failed (too radical)
  • 📊 Score: 25% success (ideas lived, but centralized)

docs.title: Napster: Killed, But Idea Lives
docs.text:
• ✅ Technology proven (P2P works)
  • ❌ Legally destroyed (sued to death)
  • 📊 Score: 25% success (replaced by centralized Spotify)

docs.title: NeXuS: Designed to Succeed
docs.text:
• ✅ Technology (building on proven foundations)
  • ✅ No single point of failure (fully distributed)
  • ✅ Incremental adoption (migration path)
  • ✅ Solves real problems (censorship, surveillance)
  • ✅ Perfect timing (privacy crisis NOW)
  • 📊 Target: 100% success

docs.title: The Manifesto
docs.text:
We are finishing what they started.

Satoshi gave us decentralized money. We're building decentralized infrastructure.

Bell Labs gave us distributed computing. We're building distributed computing with privacy.

Napster gave us peer-to-peer sharing. We're building peer-to-peer everything.

They showed us the path. NeXuS walks it to completion.

🌀 Bitcoin + Plan 9 + Napster = NeXuS 🌀

The True Vision, Finally Realized.

Decentralized. Distributed. Peer-to-Peer. Private. Together Everyone Achieves More.

Resources: - Bitcoin Whitepaper: https://bitcoin.org/bitcoin.pdf - Plan 9 Papers: http://doc.cat-v.org/plan_9/ - Napster History: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Napster - NeXuS: You're building it right now! 🚀

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Status

The network/privacy layer is ready. The economy layer (chain, wallet, DIVA) is Phase 1.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Alpine Linux (recommended) or any Linux distribution
  • 1GB RAM minimum (2GB recommended)
  • Any storage — contribution is percentage-based, no minimum
  • Internet connection (or LoRa radio for off-grid via Reticulum)

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
# Coming soon — one script deploys everything
curl -fsSL https://nexus.example/install.sh | bash

docs.title: What Gets Installed
docs.text:
See Required Applications for the full list.

The installer sets up:

  1. Transport layer — Tor bridges + I2P + Reticulum
  2. Privacy stack — all traffic routed through darknet by default
  3. Optional desktop — labwc + foot terminal + fuzzel launcher
  4. CLI toolkit — tmux, fzf, bat, ripgrep, neovim

docs.title: After Install
docs.text:
Your node is part of the mesh. No registration. No account. Your master key is your identity.

Set your contribution percentage in the Command Center (once wallet is built — Phase 1).

docs.title: nexus-docs - Action Report
docs.text: Project: /home/user/Documents/nexus-docs Created: 2026-03-09

docs.title: MkDocs site built + all docs wired in + infographics embedded (2026-03-09 19:08)
docs.text:
What: Built the NeXuS MkDocs documentation site with Material theme and wired in all docs and infographics.

Why: Need a browsable docs site for NeXuS that renders all design documents with proper navigation and images.

How: - MkDocs + mkdocs-material installed via pipx inject - Site at ~/Documents/nexus-docs/, served on localhost:8000 - 14 pages across 8 sections: What Is NeXuS, Network, Security, AI, Development, Architecture, Node, Dev - 4 infographics embedded: nexus-30kft-view-blueprint, hybrid-sovereignty, project-chimera, survival-com-system - All docs given proper H1 headers - Duplicate systems-overview-nexus.md removed - Design docs linked via symlinks so edits auto-reflect - Git initialised on nexus-docs repo

To serve: cd ~/Documents/nexus-docs && mkdocs serve

Files: mkdocs.yml, docs/ (14 pages + 4 images)

docs.title: GitHub Pages live deployment + homepage redesign (2026-03-09)
docs.text:
What: Deployed NeXuS docs to GitHub Pages at https://hackenstacks.github.io/nexus/

Why: User requested public docs hosting on GitHub with a redesigned homepage — 30k ft view, quick start, downloads, emoji/color/italic styling.

How: - Created GitHub repo hackenstacks/nexus (public) - Updated mkdocs.yml: added site_url, repo_url, emoji extension (pymdownx.emoji), attr_list, md_in_html, pymdownx.details, pymdownx.tasklist, navigation.tabs.sticky, navigation.instant - Rewrote docs/index.md: full homepage with emojis, tabbed quick start, 4-card downloads grid, split what's-working/building checklist, 8-card docs grid, philosophy block, join section - Ran mkdocs gh-deploy --force — pushes built site to gh-pages branch - GitHub Pages auto-enabled on gh-pages branch

Files: mkdocs.yml, docs/index.md

Live URL: https://hackenstacks.github.io/nexus/ (active in ~1-2 min)

To redeploy after changes: 

cd ~/Documents/nexus-docs && mkdocs gh-deploy

docs.title: MkDocs site created — all docs wired, infographics embedded (2026-03-09 19:38)
docs.text:
What: Built the NeXuS MkDocs documentation site with Material theme, wired in all existing docs and infographics.

Why: Needed a browsable, searchable docs site that renders all design documents with navigation, syntax highlighting, and embedded images.

How: - MkDocs 1.6.1 + mkdocs-material installed via pipx inject - Site root: /home/user/Documents/nexus-docs/ - docs/ contains all documentation pages - 14+ pages across 8 nav sections: What Is NeXuS, Network, Security, AI, Development, Architecture, Node, Dev - Design docs from nexus-design/ linked into docs/ directory - 4 infographics embedded: - nexus-30kft-view-blueprint.png → homepage hero - hybrid-sovereignty.png → nexus-overview.md - project-chimera.png → ai-merged-identity.md - survival-com-system.png → zombie-apoc-doc.md - All docs given proper H1 headers where missing - Duplicate systems-overview-nexus.md removed (identical to system-arch-chimera.md, confirmed by user) - User-contributed docs integrated: 3key-security, ai-merged-identity, autonomouse-ai-intelligence, cli-libs-tui-frameworks, medusa-stealth-routing, oaae-framework, zombie-apoc-doc, nexus-overview, dpfoe-ai-intelligence, event-horizon, system-arch-chimera - Git initialized on nexus-docs repo

Files: mkdocs.yml, docs/ (all pages + 4 images)

To serve locally: cd ~/Documents/nexus-docs && mkdocs serve

docs.title: GitHub Pages deployed — hackenstacks/nexus live at beta-v1 (2026-03-09 19:38)
docs.text:
What: Deployed NeXuS docs site to GitHub Pages — live at https://hackenstacks.github.io/nexus/

Why: User requested public GitHub-hosted docs with a new homepage: 30k ft view, quick start, downloads, emoji/color/italic styling. Previous docs were local only.

How:

mkdocs.yml additions: - site_url: https://hackenstacks.github.io/nexus/ - repo_url: https://github.com/hackenstacks/nexus - repo_name: hackenstacks/nexus - GitHub icon on nav bar (fontawesome/brands/github) - Social link to repo - Added markdown extensions: attr_list, md_in_html, pymdownx.details, pymdownx.emoji (twemoji), pymdownx.tasklist (custom checkboxes) - Added nav features: navigation.tabs.sticky, navigation.instant, search.share, content.code.annotate

Homepage (docs/index.md) full rewrite: - hide: [navigation, toc] — full-width homepage - Version tag in H1: # :dragon: NeXuS <small>beta-v1</small> - Hero: 30k ft blueprint image + tagline + italic subheading - 8-layer stack table with emoji per row - Quick Start: 3 tabbed options (install script / manual Alpine / container try-now) - Downloads: 4 Material grid cards (ISO placeholder, install script placeholder, container stack available now, wallet placeholder) - What's Working Now: split 2-column grid (✅ ready / 🔨 Phase 1 / 🌅 Phase 2) - Documentation: 8 grid cards covering all major doc sections - Philosophy: REVERSED comparison table in code block - Join section with contribution pathways - Footer buttons: GitHub, Network Stack, Quick Start

GitHub deployment: - Created repo: gh repo create hackenstacks/nexus --public - Git init, main branch, initial commit - git remote add origin https://github.com/hackenstacks/nexus.git - git push -u origin main — source on main branch - mkdocs gh-deploy --force — built site pushed to gh-pages branch - GitHub Pages auto-enabled on gh-pages

License question: AGPL v3 recommended — not yet added, pending user confirmation

Files: - mkdocs.yml — updated - docs/index.md — complete rewrite - GitHub repo: https://github.com/hackenstacks/nexus - Live docs: https://hackenstacks.github.io/nexus/

To redeploy: cd ~/Documents/nexus-docs && mkdocs gh-deploy
